coredata.dc:title: Performance improvement and exhaust emissions reduction in diesel engine through the use of graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanoparticles and ethanol-biodiesel blends
coredata.dc:creator: Heidari-Maleni, Aram
Mesri Gundoshmian, Tarahom
Jahanbakhshi, Ahmad
Ghobadian, Barat
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, the effects of graphene quantum dot (GQD) nano-particles on performance and emissions of a diesel engine with ethanol-biodiesel blends was investigated. Biodiesel was used in the blend of B10. The GQD nano-particles with concentration of 30 ppm were considered for each fuel blend. Experiments were performed at engine speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm. Various parameters, such as power, torque, specific fuel consumption (SFC), carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), and nitrogen oxides (NOx), were investigated. The results showed that adding GQD nanoparticles to fuel increased power and torque by 28.18% and 12.42% respectively, and reduced SFC, CO and UHC by 14.35%, 29.54% and 31.12% respectively, compared to D100 fuel. Finally, it can be concluded that GQD nano-particles can be introduced as a suitable alternative fuel additive for ethanol-biodiesel blends. Also, the adding GQD nanoparticles to fuel could be a viable alternative solution to diesel that can effectively address global energy crisis and environmental issues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diesel engine
Biodiesel
Biocompatible nanoparticles
Engine performance
Exhaust emissions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum dot synthesis and analysis
Production of biodiesel fuel and preparation of fuel samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different steps of result analysis in the present research.
Engine torque variations at different fuel consumption and engine speeds.
Engine power variations at different fuel consumption and engine speeds.
SFC variations at different fuel consumption and engine speeds.
CO emissions as affected by the different fuels under various engine speeds conditions.
CO2 emissions as affected by the different fuels under various engine speeds conditions.
UHC emissions as affected by the different fuels under various engine speeds conditions.
NOx emissions as affected by the different fuels under various engine speeds conditions.
Average engine performance parameters: (a) Torque, (b) Power and (c) SFC.
Average emission of pollutants: (a) CO, (b) CO2, (c) UHC and (d) NOx.
Technical specifications of the equipment used in GQD analysis.

row.entry: Perkin-Elmer, in USA, the range of 4000–400 cm−1
Specs EA 10 Plus energy, radiation source of Al Ka

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced.
Properties of fuels used with and without additives.
Technical specifications of the gas analyzer employed in this study.

head.ce:title: Performance improvement and exhaust emissions reduction in diesel engine through the use of graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanoparticles and ethanol-biodiesel blends

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, the effects of graphene quantum dot (GQD) nano-particles on performance and emissions of a diesel engine with ethanol-biodiesel blends was investigated. Biodiesel was used in the blend of B10. The GQD nano-particles with concentration of 30 ppm were considered for each fuel blend. Experiments were performed at engine speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm. Various parameters, such as power, torque, specific fuel consumption (SFC), carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), and nitrogen oxides (NOx), were investigated. The results showed that adding GQD nanoparticles to fuel increased power and torque by 28.18% and 12.42% respectively, and reduced SFC, CO and UHC by 14.35%, 29.54% and 31.12% respectively, compared to D100 fuel. Finally, it can be concluded that GQD nano-particles can be introduced as a suitable alternative fuel additive for ethanol-biodiesel blends. Also, the adding GQD nanoparticles to fuel could be a viable alternative solution to diesel that can effectively address global energy crisis and environmental issues.

ce:section.ce:para: Effective conversion and availability of energy is one of the most important problems affecting any country's economy. One of the main energy converters is the diesel engine, which has been taken into consideration in the agricultural and transport sectors due to some of its features such as high compression ratio, low pumping performance, low fuel consumption and high thermal efficiency. In addition, the overwhelming growth in energy consumption, the reduction of fossil fuels and the emission of pollutants have led researchers to think of alternative fuels for use in diesel engines [1–3]. One of the most common alternative fuels is biodiesel fuel, which is used in combination with diesel fuel in diesel engines to reduce the emission of exhaust pollutants [4–6]. Biodiesel is a sustainable, non-toxic and biodegradable fuel, that does not have any aromatic constituents, has a high cetane number, a high flash point and excellent lubrication performance. Despite the many positive features of biodiesel fuel, the use of pure biodiesel fuel or excessive use of it in diesel fuel mixes may cause incomplete combustion, carbon deposition, and so on [7–9].

One of the ways to reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel and the emission of exhaust pollutants is to use alcohols such as ethanol and methanol in combination with diesel-biodiesel fuel. However, some problems such as low thermal value compared to diesel fuel, stability problems when mixing with diesel fuel, low cetane number, high auto-ignition temperature and low lubrication efficiency prevent the use of alcohols alone as additives to the diesel-biodiesel fuel [10,11]. Many studies have been carried out so far on the effects of adding nanoparticles to fuels for the purpose of removing the negative effects of the biodiesel and ethanol added to diesel fuel [12,13]. The researchers found that adding nanoparticles to biodiesel fuel would reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase combustion efficiency in the engine [14,15]. Much research has also been conducted on the use of metal nanoparticles such as manganese, cerium, iron, zinc and copper as additives to fuels [16,17].

For example, Kumar et al. [18] studied the effect of fuel injection pressures (180, 210 and 240 bar) and cerium oxide nanoparticles (80 ppm) on the performance and exhaust emissions of a diesel engine. The results of their study showed that the best performance was obtained at 240 bar injection pressure with a concentration of 80 ppm nanoparticles. They also found that the addition of nanoparticles decreased the HC, NOx and soot concentrations. Raju et al. [15] examined the effect of adding alumina oxide nanoparticles (30, 60, 90 ppm) to biodiesel (B20) obtained from tamarind seed on the performance and emission characteristics of the diesel engine. They reported that the addition of alumina oxide nanoparticles increased brake thermal efficiency. There was also a significant decrease in HC and CO emissions. El-Seesy et al. [19] investigated the effect of multi walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in Jojoba methyl ester-diesel blended fuel on diesel engine characteristics. They found that the brake thermal efficiency was increased by 16% and a decrease of 15% brake specific fuel consumption. Venu and Madhavan [20] examined the effect of alumina nanoparticles (Al2 O3) on biodiesel blends, wherein the experimental results revealed reduction in BSFC, NOX, HC, and CO2 in spite of the increase in CO and smoke emission. Sajith et al. [21] were carried out an experimental investigation to study the effects of adding nanoparticles of cerium oxide on biodiesel. They were studied the impacts of increasing cerium oxide nanoparticles on engine performance, chemical-physical properties of the fuel, and emissions resulting from the fuel combustion. As a parametric study, they were applied different amounts of cerium oxide (20, 40, 60, and 80 ppm) to achieve the optimal performance. The results were indicated that the flash point and viscosity of biodiesel increase by adding cerium oxide nanoparticles. The values of NOx and UHC emissions were greatly reduced.

A review of previous research shows that the use of nanoparticles in fuel has good effects on engine performance parameters and reduction of exhaust emissions. However, the unique characteristics of nanoparticles such as being small and their chemical properties have raised many concerns about their toxicity. Since metallic nanoparticles can remain in different environments without chemical and physical changes for a long time, there are many concerns about their potential threats to human health and the environment [22,23].

Quantum Dots (QD) are nanoparticles with unique optical properties which have nowadays become very important. These nanoparticles are in fact very small nanocrystals whose size is in the 2–10 nm range and show characteristics of a semiconductor. Due to their small size, these nanoparticles have unique optical and electrical properties and electrons play a key role in the creation of such properties [24,25]. Since the discovery of graphene, the properties and applications of it have been extensively studied and exploited. However, many researchers have discovered several limitations for graphene such as zero bands, low absorption, and so on. Structural modification of graphene was investigated to overcome these shortcomings and graphene quantum dots (GQD) were developed by Ponomarenko and Geim [26]. GQD significantly changes the electron distribution. In addition, GQD have non-zero bands that are different from graphene. Increase in optical absorption of graphene due to band opening in GQD has also led to higher energy spectra. GQD features include high stability, non-toxicity, good solubility, surface bonding and biocompatibility [27]. GQD properties are superior to the properties of many traditional semiconductors. Hence, graphene quantum dots are used in many potential applications such as solar cells [28], optical images [29], bioimaging [30], fluorescent agents [31], light emitting diodes [32], batteries [33] and sensors [34].

Given the toxic effects of metallic nanoparticles on living organisms, the use of biological nanoparticles such as GQD can significantly reduce this concern [22]. GQD is one of the newest carbon-based nanoparticles, and many research studies have shown adding carbon-based nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel fuel improves performance parameters and reduces emissions of exhaust gases. Therefore, in the present study, the effect of adding biocompatible GQD nanoparticles to diesel-bioethanol fuel as well as the performance and emissions of a diesel engine were investigated. To stabilize the nanoparticles within the biodiesel fuel, bioethanol was used as the intermediate fluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dot synthesis and analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, GQD was synthesized by direct decomposition of citric acid gas through the “bottom-up” method. Then, 2 g of citric acid were placed in a microwave device for 5 min. Subsequently, liquid citric acid (orange juice) was added to absolute ethanol (made in Germany) and the mixture was subjected to ultrasound for 10 min using an ultrasonic device (400 W). After stabilizing the graphene quantum dot in ethanol, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis, high-resolution power transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), ultraviolet visible (UV) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were measured. The technical specifications of the equipment used in this study are presented in Table 1 . The analytical results of FTIR, HR-TEM, UV and XRD are also reported in Figs. 1–4
, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of biodiesel fuel and preparation of fuel samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, biodiesel was extracted from refined fish oil using trans-esterification method. The properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced are reported in Table 2 . Finally, biodiesel (B10) was mixed with GQD + bioethanol. GQD with the concentration of 30 ppm and bioethanol with 2, 4, 6 and 8 vol% concentrations were combined in biodiesel (B10) fuel. Fuel combinations include D100, B10, B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30. Table 3 presents the physical properties of the combination of bioethanol + GQD30 in bioethanol (B10) fuel.

The torque variations at different engine speeds for different fuels are given in Fig. 6 . The results show that by increasing the engine speed from 1800 to 2400 rpm, the engine torque decreases. Since cylinder filling is incomplete at high speeds, the compression pressure decreases and as a result the engine torque is reduced. In addition, fuel combustion pressure depends on the thermal value of the fuel used. In similar studies, the researchers reported that the reduction in torque was due to a decrease in engine volume efficiency at high speeds [35,36]. The results provided in Fig. 6 also show that the addition of GQD nanoparticles and bioethanol to biodiesel (B10) increases engine torque. The engine speed of 1800 rpm produced the highest engine torque in all the fuels tested. At all the engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm), the B10 + E6 + GQD30 had the highest engine torque. Different fuel combinations (B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30) increased torque at all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm) compared to diesel fuel (D100) by 9.29%, 11.18%, 14.69% and 13.18%, respectively. Moreover, different combinations of fuel increased engine torque at all engine speeds compared to biodiesel (B10), by 10.24%, 12.15%, 15.69 and 14.69% respectively. The use of biodiesel (B10) resulted to the lowest torque over all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm) compared to other fuels.

Fig. 7 shows power changes at different engine speeds using different fuels. The results show that the B10 + E4 + GQD30 fuel has the best performance in power output at all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm). Also, with increase in engine torque for all fuels, the engine power showed an almost increasing trend. Since engine power is proportional to the product of the torque and the engine speed, the maximum engine power depends not only on the explosion force but also on the engine speed. Due to the test conditions, change in engine power will be affected by the engine torque.

As can be seen in Fig. 7, adding GQD nanoparticles and bioethanol to biodiesel fuel (B10), have led to improvement in engine performance by increasing power. Engine power in different fuel combinations (B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30) and at all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm) increased compared to diesel fuel (D100) for 10.26%, 20.53%, 13.56% and 14.89%, respectively. Percentage and proportion of biodiesel (B10) increased for 17.36%, 26.79%, 19.60% and 21.00% respectively.

The specific fuel consumption rates at different engine speeds using different fuels are shown in Fig. 8 . According to the results of Fig. 8, using the B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel has reduced SFC compared to other fuels. The results also show that as the engine speed increases, the SFC also increases. At higher engine speeds, SFC increased because of the low braking power and lower fuel consumption at high speed ranges. Specific fuel consumption is a function of fuel braking power. In other words, whenever the fuel braking power is increased, SFC reduction will occur. Addition of GQD nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel fuel reduced the SFC. The reason is that adding GQD nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel fuels improves combustion and increases engine power, thereby reducing SFC. In similar studies conducted before, researchers reported that decrease in SFC caused by adding nanoparticles to the fuel could be attributed to faster combustion, higher heat release rate, more complete combustion and lower viscosity, which results in increased engine power [37,38].

In comparison with diesel fuel (D100), SFC levels in different fuel combinations (B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30) and at all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm) decreased for 11.62%, 15.49%, 15.24% and 13.55 respectively and they decreased for 6.20%, 10.30%, 10.04% and 8.24% compared to biodiesel fuel (B10).

Fig. 9 shows the CO emission from the combustion of different fuels at different engine speeds. The results in Fig. 9 show that CO emissions for biodiesel fuel (B10) are lower than those of the diesel fuel (D100) at all engine speeds (1800 to 2400 rpm) because the amount of oxygen in diesel fuel is zero while biodiesel fuel has 10 to 12 wt% of oxygen. Therefore, the combustion mechanism of biodiesel fuel is better than that of the diesel fuel and reduces CO emission. The lowest and highest CO emissions at all engine speeds are B10 + E8 + GQD30 and diesel (D100), respectively. Also in all treatments the lowest CO emission was related to 1800 rpm engine speed and the highest CO emission was related to 2400 rpm engine speed. On average, CO emissions for the B10 + E8 + GQD30 fuel compared to diesel (D100) and biodiesel fuels (B10) decreased by 29.54% and 19.83% respectively, which the reason for this could be due to the presence of GQD nanoparticles in the fuel. In similar studies, researchers reported that the use of nanoparticles and bioethanol mixed with fuels due to rapid and complete combustion of fuel and the presence of oxygen in them reduces CO emissions [39,40].

Fig. 10 shows the effect of engine speed and fuel type on CO2 emissions. CO2 emissions are higher in fuel mixtures (B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30) than in the diesel (D100) and biodiesel (B10) fuels, due to the complete combustion of fuel in the engine. These results indicate the superiority of combining GQD nanoparticles with bioethanol fuel (at 2, 4, 6 and 8%, vol) over using a single biodiesel fuel (B10). In similar previous studies, researchers reported that CO2 gas is produced when the combustion is complete in the engine [41,42].

The highest CO2 emissions are related to B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel which at speeds 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm compared to diesel fuel (D100) are 20.41%, 25% and 27.55%, respectively, and compared to biodiesel (B10) increased 19.17%, 23.07% and 26.26%, respectively.

The effect of engine speed and fuel type on UHC emission is reported in Fig. 11 . The results show that at all engine speeds, B10 + E8 + GQD30 fuel had the lowest UHC emission. Also, the addition of GQD and bioethanol (at 2, 4, 6, and 8%, vol) to biodiesel (B10) reduced UHC emissions at all engine speeds. The high catalytic activity of nanoparticles increases the surface area to volume ratio and thus results in an increase in the energy produced inside the cylinder improving the chemical processes and leading to more complete fuel combustion and reduced emission of pollutants. Researchers have reported in similar research studies that adding nanoparticles to a diesel-biodiesel fuel blend reduced the release of UHC due to rapid and complete combustion of the fuel [43–45]. The UHC emission reductions at speed of 1800 rpm for B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel compared to D100 and B10 are 33.51% and 15.45%, respectively. B10 + E8 + GQD30 fuel at speeds of 2100 and 2400 rpm compared to D100 fuel with 36.41% and 39.20% and B10 fuel with 17.60% and 30.96% contributed to UHC emission reduction, respectively.

NOx is produced from the reaction of nitrogen and oxygen gases in the air during combustion, especially at high temperatures. In areas of high motor vehicle traffic, such as in large cities, the amount of nitrogen oxidesemittedinto the atmosphere as air pollution can be significant. Fig. 12 shows the emission rate of NOx due to the effect of different engine speeds and fuel type. The results indicate that D100 fuel had the lowest NOx emission at all engine speeds compared to the other fuels. B10 fuel also has the highest NOx emissions compared to other fuels. The majority of the published literature are unanimous in associating biodiesel application with increased NOx emissions [46]. Some attributes of biodiesel like unsaturated compounds and active atom oxygen content could explain its higher NOx formation in comparison with neat diesel [47].

The average performance parameters of the engine are reported in Fig. 13 . The results show that the maximum torque was when using B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel. B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel increased engine torque by 14.69% and 15.69% compared to D100 and B10 fuels, respectively (Fig. 13-a). Engine power reached its maximum when using B10 + E4 + GQD30 fuel, which increased 20.53% and 26.89%, respectively, compared to D100 and B10 fuels (Fig. 13-b). Also, B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuel decreased SFC by 15.24% and 10.04% compared to D100 and B10, respectively (Fig. 13-c).

Fig. 14 shows the average emission of pollutants from the engine. The results show that the combination of different fuels with GQD significantly reduces CO emissions, with an average reduction of 29.54% and 19.83%, respectively, compared to D100 and B10 fuels (Fig. 14-a). The highest CO2 emissions for B10 + E6 + GQD30 fuels are 24.35% and 15.94% compared to D100 and B10 fuels, respectively (Fig. 14-b). The minimum UHC emission is related to B10 + E8 + GQD30 fuels with a reduction of 36.60% and 22.30% compared to D100 and B10 fuels, respectively (Fig. 14-c). Also, B10 fuel had the highest share of NOx emissions, up 1.29% over D100 fuel (Fig. 14-d).

In this work, biodiesel produced from fish oil was mixed in various ratio with ethanol-biodiesel blends and GQD nano-particles. Thermo-physical and chemical properties such as viscosity, density and flash point of all fuel blends have been determined. The engine performance and exhaust emission parameters have been investigated in a single cylinder direct injection diesel engine. According to the results of this study, the following conclusions can be given below:
1-
Adding GQD nanoparticles to the fuel increased the power and torque of the engine. On average, compared to diesel fuel (D100), adding GQD nanoparticles to B10 fuel increased engine power and torque by 12.42% and 28.18% respectively.
2-
Addition of GQD nanoparticles to B10 fuel at all engine speeds reduced the SFC compared to B10 and D100 fuels. On average, at all engine speeds, SFC was reduced in mixed fuels (B10 + E2 + GQD30, B10 + E4 + GQD30, B10 + E6 + GQD30 and B10 + E8 + GQD30) by 14.35% compared to diesel fuel (D100).
3-
In addition, compared to diesel fuel (D100), the amounts of CO and UHC emissions were reduced by adding GQD to the fuel by 29.54% and 31.12%, respectively.
4-
As an overall conclusion, it can be reported that GQD nanoparticle can improve the performance and emission of a diesel engine fuelled with the diesel-biodiesel blends.

Aram Heidari Maleni: Project administration, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Tarahom Mesri Gundoshmian: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Ahmad Jahanbakhshi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Barat Ghobadian: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
